title: Nvidia Blackwell GB200 NVL72 & Networking | by Jonathan Hui | Medium
text:
Nvidia Blackwell GB200 NVL72 & Networking
One might think that winning the AI accelerator market requires designing the most powerful accelerators and memory systems. However, for AI models with trillions of parameters, this alone is not enough. Training such large models may require hundreds of thousands of GPUs.
Success depends on building a high-performance distributed system that scales efficiently and almost linearly with the increasing number of GPUs. To succeed, several factors are critical: how effectively GPUs are interconnected, and advancements in interconnect technologies that offer low latency and high bandwidth between GPUs and compute nodes. Just as GPUs are designed with domain-specific optimizations, networks must also follow an AI-first design. The design starts with the algorithms and workloads, rather than prioritizing the network itself first. Co-design between hardware and software engineers is crucial. Additionally, total cost of ownership (TCO) is a key consideration, encompassing power consumption, cooling requirements, and potential needs for new infrastructure, such as rack and data center redesigns. A full software ecosystem is equally crucial, ensuring seamless integration of machine learning frameworks, libraries, and tools to optimize both performance and scalability.
Disclaimer
Both the B300 and Vera Rubin platforms have been announced. As I am in the process of writing a book on deep learning, this article requires some updates and revisions. Fortunately, much of the core content remains valid, so I have decided to release it. In addition, there are several network topologies discussed here that are quite interesting but will not be covered in the book.
For readers who may be interested, the book has taken a significant amount of effort to write, and I have spent considerable time on topics that are truly worth studying. If you would like to be notified when it is released, please connect with me on LinkedIn.
NVLink & NVLink Switch
NVLink is a high-speed interconnect technology designed for direct GPU-to-GPU communication. It offers significantly higher communication bandwidth and lower latency compared to the traditional PCIe bus. The Blackwell architecture uses the fifth generation of NVLink, with 18 NVLink ports (connections) per GPU and each port providing 50 GB/s of bandwidth in both directions, resulting in a total bidirectional bandwidth of 1.8 TB/s (0.9 TBs for each direction). This enables communication speeds over 14 times faster than PCIe Gen5.
NVLink uses point-to-point communication, which can lead to unnecessary separate connections. For example, in tensor parallelism, a model layer’s computation is split across multiple GPUs. Once a GPU completes its portion, it must send the results to all other GPUs. Because of NVLink’s point-to-point nature, a separate connection must be established with each GPU. In an 8-GPU system, this means a GPU must send the same data seven times, leading to inefficient bandwidth usage and increased latency.
To address the point-to-point communication issue, switches are added between GPUs to eliminate unnecessary connections and provide dedicated communication pathways that are not impacted by other connections. Built on top of NVLink, NVLink Switches offer this solution, though at a higher system cost. These switches use a crossbar architecture to create a non-blocking topology, allowing any GPU to communicate with another without interference from other connections. This ensures that each GPU can connect to any other at full bandwidth, enabling simultaneous communication among all GPUs, regardless of the number of GPUs in the system. For instance, in tensor parallelism, each GPU processes its part of the computation and sends its partial result to a switch just once. The switch then broadcasts the result to all other GPUs in the system. In this process, Nvidia’s Collective Communications Library (NCCL) is used to optimize communication between GPUs, facilitating efficient data exchange and synchronization during distributed computing tasks.
Nvidia GB200 NVL72
The GB200 NVL72 is a reference design intended to accelerate AI and HPC workloads. It allows third parties, such as system integrators and data center providers, to build large-scale AI infrastructure based on Nvidia’s validated blueprint.
The GB200 NVL72 features a rack configuration with 18 compute trays (18 dual GB200 compute nodes), interconnected via 9 NVLink Switch Trays. Each rack in this system operates with a 120kW power load, significantly higher than the typical ~10kW per rack in a conventional data center. While this configuration offers the advantage of a minimal footprint, the substantial increase in power density may slow down the adoption of this form factor due to infrastructure upgrade requirements. The high power density also requires liquid cooling for the system, which helps reduce overall power consumption by lowering the demand on fans.
Each compute tray is equipped with two GB200 Grace Blackwell Superchips, each containing two B200 GPUs and one Grace CPU, all connected through a 900GB/s NVLink chip-to-chip interconnect. NVL72 includes a total of 36 Grace CPUs and 72 Blackwell GPUs.
A single Grace CPU consists of 72 Arm Neoverse V2 cores and supports up to 480GB of LPDDR5X memory. The Blackwell-architecture GPU, featuring 208 billion transistors, are manufactured using TSMC’s custom-built 4NP process, a specialized 4-nanometer manufacturing technology. Due to constraints on die size, each GPU is composed of two dies connected by a 10 TB/s chip-to-chip link, creating a unified GPU with 192GB HBM3e memory and 8TB/s memory bandwidth.
Each GPU features 18 NVLink ports, and with 4 GPUs per node, this results in a total of 72 NVLink ports per node. To interconnect all the nodes in the GB200 NVL72 system, 9 NVLink Switch trays are required. Each tray houses 2 NVLink chips and offers a total of 144 NVLink ports, with each port capable of delivering 100 GB/s of bandwidth. Each B200 GPU will have a single connection with an NVLink Switch.
NVLink is an inter-chip communication technology that functions and operates much like a memory system. In a 72-GPU rack configuration, each GPU can directly access the memory of any other GPU as if it were accessing its own local memory. The NVLink Switch interconnects each GPU pair at 1.8 TB/s and supports full all-to-all communication across the system. With near-linear scalability, the rack functions like a single GPU, delivering 1.4 exaflops of AI performance and providing 30TB of high-speed memory accessible to all its components. The 4th generation NVLink Switch is the first rack-level switch chip capable of supporting up to 576 fully connected GPUs in a non-blocking communication fabric.
To host 576 GPUs, it may require using two racks for each set of 72 GPUs instead of just one. This setup enables the integration of additional switches for efficient GPU-to-GPU communication.
The diagram below illustrates a potential configuration in which each rack contains 9 NVLink switch trays and 18 compute trays, with each compute tray hosting a single GB200 Superchip rather than the dual-chip setup.
This arrangement frees up half of the ports in each NVLink Leaf Switch tray, allowing them to connect to the spine switches for inter-rack communication. NVLink Switch Tray 1 ports in the leaf switches connect to Plane 1 NVLink switches, Tray 2 ports connect to Plane 2, and so on. As a result, moving data between GPUs across different racks will require only two hops through NVLink switches. This configuration provides a higher CPU-to-GPU core ratio and potentially more memory per GPU, which can improve the performance of recommendation models that rely on massive embedding tables and require more CPU-driven operations.
The diagram below presents an alternative configuration, where each compute tray continues to host two GB200 Superchips, but the number of compute trays is reduced from 18 to 9 per rack.
While Google’s TPUs use fiber optics to enhance inter-node communication, especially for higher data transfer rates over long distances, Nvidia employs a selective approach in the GB200 NVL72 system. This system densely interconnects nodes via a copper cable cartridge for leaf switch connections, which is significantly more power-efficient. However, for longer-distance communication, such as between leaf and spine switches, Nvidia relies on optical connections to maintain high bandwidth and low latency across the system.
